passages.text: SECTION 7 Pain 1546 treated with PNS; from their cohort, all treated patients suf- fered less pain and enjoyed a better quality of life thereafter. Finally, Verrills et al. reported on 100 patients who underwent PNS for chronic pain including craniofacial, thoracic, lumbo- sacral, abdominal, pelvic, and groin pain. Patients enrolled in the study demonstrated an average pain reduction of 4.2 ± 2.5 pain scale points on an 11-point scale following PNS, and 72% of patients reported significantly decreased analgesic use at an average 8.1-month follow-up.32 Within the realm of treating postoperative pain with PNS, Stinson et al. reported on three cases of patients with intractable postoperative inguinal pain, and all subjects had 75% to 100% pain relief up to 12 months postimplantation.15 Likewise, small case series have demonstrated the successful use of PNS for pain control following rotator cuff repair and a significant reduction in the need for opioids among the postoperative orthopedic trauma patients who underwent PNS.25,26 Gilmore et al. carried out a multicenter, double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial in an amputee population (N = 28) with chronic postamputation neuropathic pain in which all patients were implanted with fine wire leads in close proximity to the sciatic and femoral nerves. One-half of the patients underwent active peripheral nerve stimulation for the first 4 weeks of the trial, and the others received sham stimulation (no stimulation with leads in place), with crossover of the placebo group at 4 weeks. The primary outcome of the study, >50% pain relief at 4 weeks, was met in the treatment arm but not in the placebo arm. Notably, neither group met >50% pain relief in the placebo-crossover group 8 weeks into the study, which was 4 weeks after their initiation of active stimulation. Although both groups underwent ultrasound-guided implantation of the leads, the placebo group never underwent stimulation testing, whereas the treatment arm did.33 Thus, the trial supported the use of PNS for amputation- associated neuropathic pain and highlighted the importance that stimulation testing be performed for optimal lead placement. Although complication rates are generally low, reported complications of the aforementioned studies include lead migration, fracture, or misplacement, as well as infection, stimulation intolerance, and potential nerve damage. DORSAL ROOT GANGLION STIMULATION A relative of PNS and spinal cord stimulation, dorsal root gan- glion (DRG) stimulation represents a relatively new player in the field of neuromodulation and has garnered attention for its potential to effectively treat CRPS of the lower limbs. DRG stimulation is similar to spinal cord stimulation in principal but is focused on stimulating the DRG as opposed to the dor- sal columns of the spinal cord. The lead for DRG stimulation is specialized to provide percutaneous entry through the epi- dural space, exiting through the foramen, and resting around the DRG. The ACCURATE study, a randomized, controlled multicenter trial, evaluated DRG stimulation compared with SCS stimulation for the treatment of chronic, intractable pain of the lower limbs attributed to CRPS or causalgia in 152 patients. The study demonstrated superior pain relief after 12 months with DRG stimulation as compared with spinal cord stimulation. Specifically, 74.2% of subjects experienced meaningful pain relief with DRG stimulation, compared with 53% who received traditional spinal cord stimulation. One- third had more than 80% pain relief and no tingling sensa- tion, which is a common side effect with traditional spinal cord stimulation.34 DRG stimulation is being explored for treat- ment of additional pain etiologies, including but not limited to phantom limb pain, with its degree of efficacy to be elucidated in the near future. Further discussion of DRG stimulation is beyond the scope of this chapter

passages.text: CHAPTER 461 Microsurgery for Cerebral Cavernous Malformations 3771 461 These exceptions are made based on the potential effects of hem- orrhage and the perceived surgical risks of resection. Patients with asymptomatic lobar and cerebellar lesions with no relation to eloquent cortex are generally at low surgical risk, and they can be offered resection versus observation. Incidentally discov- ered periventricular lesions that have an exophytic component extending into the ventricle are typically recommended for surgi- cal resection for two reasons. First, the ventricle offers no tissue pressure in the case of rupture, and devastating intraventricular hemorrhage can result. Second, the exophytic portion of the cavernoma provides direct access to the lesion without travers- ing normal tissue. Special consideration must be given for exo- phytic lesions on the floor of the fourth ventricle. When these lesions happen to be covered by even a thin tissue layer, we do not recommend myelotomy. Brainstem lesions in asymptomatic patients may be considered for resection in cases in which the lesions reach the pial surface. Symptomatic cavernomas tend to be managed more aggressively. Patients with epileptic seizures should be considered for lesionectomy if their seizure focus localizes to the cavernous malformation. Surgical extirpation is typically recommended for patients with lobar and cerebellar lesions with hemorrhage (Fig. 461.3). Lesions located in eloquent cortex should be evaluated with fMRI, and resection should be tailored appropriately. If presenting symptoms are mild and the lesion localizes within eloquent cortex, observation can be considered. However, repeat hemorrhages demand reevaluation for surgical resection. Patients with deep-seated supratentorial malformations and brainstem cavernomas should be recommended for resection in the case of repeat hemorrhages with progressive neurological decline. Single hemorrhages in these locations should be managed conservatively unless the cavernoma reaches a pial surface. Surgical intervention after a hemorrhagic presentation should be delayed for at least 5 days when possible to allow the clot to liquefy. Rarely, symptomatic progression requires emergency decompression. Patients with severe medical comorbidities should not be offered surgical intervention. Other contraindications include deep-seated or brainstem cavernous malformations with only a single hemorrhage that do not have any representation at a pial surface. passages.text: CHAPTER 164 Pineal Tumors 1151 164 Tissue Diagnosis: Biopsy Versus Open Resection Given the diverse pathology that can occur in the pineal region, a histologic diagnosis is necessary to guide management deci- sions, which are varied.39,42,48,85,94–97 Although CSF cytology and radiographic examination provide insight into the histologic diagnosis, they are not sufficiently sensitive to supplant a tissue diagnosis.72,73,96–102 CSF cytology occasionally reveals malignant cells but is rarely diagnostic. As mentioned earlier, the only time that a tissue diagnosis is precluded is when malignant germ cell markers are present. In this case, chemotherapy and radiotherapy should be pursued straightaway.73,102,103 Tissue diagnosis can be achieved via stereotactic or endoscopic needle biopsies or through an open surgical procedure. Clinicians may advocate for one or another of these approaches as a standard. However, understanding the potential merits and disadvantages of each approach is critical to customizing care to the individual patient rather than relying on clinical dogma. The decision to pursue simple biopsy versus open surgery is influenced by the clinical features of the patient, radiographic features of the tumor, and the surgeon’s degree of experience with the procedures. The advantage of open resection is the ability to obtain larger amounts of tissue and more extensive tissue sampling. This is particularly important for pineal region lesions in general and germ cell tumors in particular because heterogeneity and mixed cell populations are common (Fig. 164.5; Table 164.3).24,94,104–109 Indeed, several studies have suggested sampling errors of roughly 18% to 21% when comparing initial pathology on stereotactic biopsy and final pathology on open resection.109,110 Open resection provides the benefit of tumor debulking or complete resection. For one-third of benign tumors, resection is complete and curative, thus making open surgery the procedure of choice (Table 164.4).39,94,111 Although the advantages of tumor debulking are less apparent with malignant tumors, anecdotal evidence suggests that radical resection does improve response to adjuvant therapy.33,94,111–114 Additionally, aggressive debulking can reduce mass effect on critical structures; relieve obstructive hydrocephalus, obviating the need for permanent CSF diversion; and reduce the risk for postoperative hemorrhage into an incompletely resected tumor bed.24,94,111 Open procedures, on the other hand, confer higher short-term surgical morbidity than stereotactic needle biopsies, although this short-term disadvantage may be balanced by the long-term advantage of tumor control. Stereotactic biopsy, conversely, offers the advantages of relative ease of performance and reduced complications when compared with an open procedure.23,27,115–117 However, it is important to remember that the pineal region is one of the most hazardous locations to safely perform stereotactic biopsy given surrounding structures, and extreme care must be taken in target and trajectory planning. Stereotactic biopsy carries a risk for hemorrhage from several mechanisms including bleeding in highly vascular tumors, damage to the deep venous system, and bleeding into the ventricle, where tissue turgor is insufficient to tamponade minor bleeding.24,94,104,116 These risks notwithstanding, several large series have validated the relative safety of stereotactic biopsy for pineal tumors despite their rather hazardous location.23,115 A retrospective multicenter study of stereotactic biopsies in 370 patients reported a mortality rate of 1.3%.117 Of minor concern but worth mentioning is a report of metastatic seeding along the biopsy tract after biopsy of a pineoblastoma.118 Flexible endoscopes have made neuroendoscopic biopsy a more attractive option, allowing surgeons to perform a third ventriculostomy and biopsy through the same bur hole. Bleeding risks and sampling bias are concerns, similar to stereotactic biopsy. passages.text: 2035 265 CHAPTER 265 Thoracolumbar Spinal Disorders in Pediatric Patients bracing or casting is warranted if the curve progresses by 5 degrees or more. Serial molded body casts are typically used for an infant up to 18 months of age. Beyond this age, the infant is placed in a brace, often a Milwaukee brace, to be worn at least 23 hours per day. If the curve ultimately corrects, relapse during adolescence is very unlikely.108 Surgical Management (Nonfusion) In children with early-onset scoliosis, bracing and casting are traditionally the preferred treatment approaches with moderate curves. If these approaches are contraindicated or fail, surgical treatment may be indicated. Nonfusion instrumentation tech- niques attempt to control spinal deformity while still allowing for spinal growth through serial lengthening procedures, until the child reaches an adequate age and skeletal maturity for defini- tive fusion.109 There are currently four general approaches for nonfusion instrumentation, as follows: (1) single growing rod with a claw foundation placed proximally or distally,110,111 (2) dual growing rods with claw foundations placed proximally and distally,111,112 (3) VEPTR,36,37,113-117 and (4) vertebral growth modulation techniques such as tethering and stapling.118-120 Anterior vertebral body tethering is a promising fusionless treat- ment option for skeletally immature patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis.118,119 Studies by Samdani et al.118,119 dem- onstrated that patients treated with this growth-modulation technique experienced curve improvement with minimal major complications. The authors acknowledged that longer-term follow-up is still needed. The Growing Spine Committee of the Scoliosis Research Society validated a new classification for early- onset scoliosis that may help promote further understanding of this diverse condition and its management.98,99 Further details of nonfusion procedures, such as magnetically controlled growing rods, for management of early-onset scoliosis are discussed in detail in Chapter 349. Surgical Management (Fusion) Information in the following sections is also presented with illus- trative case examples in Video 265.3. Briefly, surgery for ado- lescent idiopathic scoliosis is often recommended in growing children when curves reach 45 to 50 degrees. Surgery is also rec- ommended in skeletally mature teenagers for curves that exceed 50 degrees because curves of this magnitude have significant risk of progression in adult life.121-123 The goals of surgery include preventing curve progression, restoring alignment, balancing the spine in all anatomic planes, and minimizing the number of verte- bral levels that are fused. Additionally, surgery provides improved cosmesis, an achievement that can be a significant consideration for patients in this age group. Current posterior instrumenta- tion systems include hooks, wires, and/or pedicle screws that are typically connected with dual rods. Despite initial concerns about safety, it appears that use of pedicle screws in pediatric patients, in experienced hands, is safe.124-128 The posterior approach for pedicle screw instrumentation has now become the mainstay of treatment when surgical fusion is indicated.129–133 Traditionally, an anterior approach was commonly used to reduce fusion levels, perform an anterior release, and avoid crankshaft phenomena. However, with current instrumentation techniques, the poste- rior approach with three-column pedicle screw fixation allows application of significant cantilever forces and torque for derota- tion maneuvers that obviate the need for an anterior procedure. Also, the anterior procedure may be associated with pulmonary decline. A recent study by Yaszay et al.134 demonstrated progres- sive decline in pulmonary function 5 years postoperatively in patients who underwent anterior instrumentation for surgical correction of adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. In the same study, patients who underwent posterior instrumentation had stable pulmonary function.134 Regardless of the instrumentation sys- tem selected, the ultimate success of the procedure depends on achieving solid bony fusion. passages.text: SECTION 11 Spine 2853.e18 the majority of studies reporting an incidence of 2% to 40%.305 The incidence of PJF ranges between 4.9% and 5.6%, with failures occurring mainly at the thoracolumbar junction.304,311 This low incidence is likely underestimated because many studies combine PJK and PJF owing to lack of standardized, uniform definitions. PJK can be observed as early as 8 weeks postoperatively, with 66% and 80% of PJK being diagnosed between 3 and 18 months after surgery, respectively.310,312,313 PJF typically occurs in the early postoperative period303 and may occur as a result of increased motion and loads on the proximal mobile segment adjacent to the UIV of a long fusion or from reciprocal changes in the unfused segments of the spine.303,311 Many factors contribute to the occurrence of PJK, including disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex, progressive deformity from aging, fractures, arthrodesis failure, degenerative disk disease, and/or facet violations.298,305,311,314,315 Thoracolumbar PJFs are more likely secondary fractures, whereas upper thoracic failures are more likely to occur secondary to soft tissue failure.311 There are several risk factors for PJK that have been identified. These factors include age above 55 years, severe preoperative sagittal imbalance, greater curvature correction, anterior and posterior spinal fusion, and extension of fusion to the lower lumbar region or the sacrum.305 In addition, a very positive sagittal imbalance combined with smaller paraspinal muscles at the UIV has been shown as a strong predictor of PJK.316 Osteopenia and high BMI are considered to be less well-established risk factors.304,305,313 The influence of the number of instrumented segments and the location of the UIV on the development of PJK is unclear; it has been reported in both low and high numbers of instrumented segments.301,317 In addition, it has also been demonstrated in both upper and lower thoracic locations of the UIV.308,317,318 Some preventive strategies have been identified with mixed results.305 Vertebral cement augmentation of the UIV+1 or UIV+2 has been used in an attempt to prevent PJK and PJF.307,319 Hybrid constructs of pedicle screws and hooks at the end of the construct have been used to provide a dynamic instead of a rigid construct and therefore prevent PJK.310 Additional techniques that have been used in the prevention of PJK include sublaminar bands,320,321 interspinous ligament augmentation with sublaminar cables,322 and the use of a polyethylene “tether” through the spinous processes.323 Although it is unclear which patients with PJK require revision surgery, there are some factors that seem to influence this decision. These include a traumatic etiology of PJF, severity of PJK angulation, higher sagittal vertical axis, and female sex.324 Clinical evaluation is done as described previously for postoperative spinal instability and deformity. We perform cephalad construct extension and kyphotic correction if there is neurological deficit, myelopathy, radiographic deformity with instability, medical refractory pain, and/or disfiguring abnormal posture. Proximal junctional Cobb angle and sagittal vertical axis are measured to assess the amount of correction needed. We assess the rigidity of the spine either during the preoperative clinical examination, by placing the patient in the prone position on the examination table and manipulating the spine, or by radiographic interpretation of the preoperative imaging obtained with the patient lying on a bolster. Depending on the flexibility of the kyphotic deformity, we plan on performing multiple Smith- Petersen osteotomies for the flexible kyphotic deformity, pedicle subtraction osteotomies for less flexible curves, and vertebral column resection for nonflexible spines or for spondylolisthesis. We perform rib osteotomies to maximize the corrective benefit of the osteotomies, especially the Smith-Petersen osteotomies. Surgery was required in 15.6% of patients with a curve magnitude less than 20 degrees, compared with 75% of patients with a curve magnitude of 21 to 30 degrees and 100% of patients with a curve magnitude exceeding 31 degrees. Finally, in a series reported by Mannherz and coworkers,52 only 6 of 43 patients remained stable with observation. Furthermore, of the 31 patients treated with bracing, 26 had curve progression with 13 ultimately requiring surgical fusion. These series demonstrate that the natural history of juvenile idiopathic scoliosis is much more aggressive than that of the adolescent form; therefore strict clinical follow-up every 4 to 6 months is warranted. Traditionally, observation is indicated for patients with curves less than 20 degrees at presentation, bracing for patients presenting with 25- to 30-degree curves, and surgical fusion for patients with curves greater than 30 to 40 degrees.40 In skeletally immature patients, growth modulation techniques such as vertebral body stapling and the bone-ligament tether can also be considered; they are discussed later. Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS), or a lateral curvature of the spine of greater than 10 degrees without an underlying abnormal- ity, is the most common pediatric spinal deformity in patients 10 to 17 years of age, with a female sex predilection 10-fold greater than that in males.53 The prevalence of AIS has been reported to range from 0.5 to 5 per 100 adolescents.1,54,55 However, only 5% of the patients diagnosed with a curve greater than 10 degrees prog- ress to a curve greater than 30 degrees.56,57 Although this disor- der is termed idiopathic, numerous studies have demonstrated an increased incidence of scoliosis within families and between mono- zygotic twins; however, no distinct autosomal or sex-linked inheri- tance pattern has been found.58-61 The current proposed etiology for idiopathic scoliosis is therefore thought to be a combination of deficiencies in either vertebral growth, spine-associated tissue (muscle, ligament, disk complex) abnormalities, or central nervous system input into paraspinal musculature. No one deficiency, how- ever, has proved to be the central cause of idiopathic scoliosis. Classification and surgical management of AIS have been addressed by two major systems. First, in 1983, King and associates62 devised a treatment algorithm–based classification system that depended on five curve types. However, major limitations of this system included inclusion of only thoracic curves, its assessment of scoliosis as a purely coronal deformity as opposed to the three- dimensional deformity inherent in scoliosis, and its poor interrater and intrarater reliability.63,64 In 2001, a new AIS classification system was developed by Lenke and colleagues65 that tried to address some of the shortcomings of the older King system (Fig. 264.3). This system encompassed most curve types, had a focus on the three-dimensional nature of scoliosis with a large focus on sagittal imbalance, and had much improved interrater and intrarater reliability.65 A thorough understanding of this system is necessary for the appropriate management of patients with AIS, and careful review of the Lenke classification system is recommended. An important lesson from this system was the understanding that spinal instrumentation and fusion should include only the major and structural minor curves. Structural curves were defined as nonflexible curves on bending and were termed major if they encompassed the largest Cobb angle. Minor structural curves were not the major curve and on bending continued to have a Cobb angle greater than 25 degrees, a thoracic kyphosis greater than 20 degrees, or a thoracolumbar kyphosis greater than 25 degrees (Video 264.2). passages.text: 969 80 • Surgical Management of Cranial Dural Arteriovenous Fistulas of transarterial embolization of the feeding arteries is usu- ally effective in symptom control and is associated with fewer complications compared with curative transvenous embolization or surgery. All patients should be followed up clinically and radiographically, and any change in symp- toms (deterioration or improvement) should prompt repeat conventional angiography to look for development of CVR (Fig. 80-5). DURAL ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULAS WITH CORTICAL VENOUS REFLUX (AGGRESSIVE FISTULAS: BORDEN TYPES II AND III, COGNARD TYPES IIB THROUGH V) These lesions must be treated aggressively because they are associated with a 15% annual risk of hemorrhage and neurologic deficit.20 Treatment should be performed soon after the diagnosis has been made.4 There is evidence that rebleeding may occur early after an initial hemorrhagic presentation. In one series, 35% of patients had a rebleed within 2 weeks after the first hemorrhage.50 Complete oblit- eration of the fistula is ideal but not critical because there is increasing evidence that disconnection of CVR alone results in long-term protection against hemorrhage and neurologic deficit.4,40-43,51 Treatment of cranial DAVFs with CVR requires a mul- tidisciplinary approach. A combination of transarterial embolization, transvenous embolization, and surgery may be required in some cases. The primary goal of treatment, whether endovascular or surgical, is to eliminate CVR. Endovascular embolization is usually the primary treat- ment modality and often begins with transarterial embo- lization. This is rarely curative and is usually followed by transvenous embolization. If endovascular therapy is not feasible or fails, then surgery is indicated. For lesions with CVR only without any sinusal drainage (Borden type III and Cognard types III, IV, and V), CVR disconnection alone is all that is required. passages.text: 982 17.6 Surgery for Intracerebral Hemorrhage Surgical options include craniotomy, minimally invasive techniques for clot evacuation, and ven­ triculostomy. Ventriculostomy is covered below in the Specific Situations section. A Brief History of Surgery for ICH Craniotomy and evacuation of major supratento­ rial ICH with impending herniation and life-­ threatening posterior fossa ICH have been standard care for many years and will likely remain so. The surgical treatment of ICH in patients who are relatively stable, however, has evolved considerably over the last 3 decades. A straightforward small retrospective study pub­ lished in 1993 by Joseph Broderick and col­ leagues at the University of Cincinnati found that mortality with ICH is significantly higher for clot volumes greater than 30 mL compared to smaller clots [143]. This finding led to a convention in neurosurgery to consider early surgical ­evacuation for ICHs >30 mL, on the grounds that mortality is higher for that group. Hence, surgery for clot evacuation to minimize the chance of the devel­ opment of clinically significant perihematoma edema and further mass effect became a widely accepted option. The Surgical Trial in Intracerebral Hemorrhage (STICH) was a multi­ center randomized trial comparing craniotomy to medical therapy for ICH; although no benefit was found overall, there was a trend toward a signifi­ cant benefit for patients with a clot near the corti­ cal surface [144]. The STICH II trial also randomized ICH patients between craniotomy and medical therapy, but included only patients with a clot within 1 cm of the cortical surface and also found no benefit with surgery [145]. Disappointing results with craniotomy inspired the development of several alternative surgical techniques for clot evacuation that are less inva­ sive than craniotomy, in hopes that less invasive techniques will provide a net benefit with clot evacuation. Minimally invasive techniques include infusion of alteplase into the clot (MISTIE) and early surgery with a brain-friendly retractor (ENRISH). Overall about 20% of spon­ taneous ICH patients have surgery, despite a lack of level I evidence to support surgery for most patients. STICH (Surgical Trial in Intracerebral Hemorrhage) [144] A total of 1033 patients with spontaneous ICH with a minimum clot diameter of 2 cm were ran­ domized to early surgery (n = 503) or initial con­ servative treatment (n = 530). Primary endpoint was Glasgow outcome scale score at 6 months. Randomization was done within 72 h of initial CT showing ICH. Patients randomized to early surgery underwent clot evacuation (via a variety of techniques including craniotomy or endos­ copy) within 24 h of randomization. Among patients randomized to initial conservative treat­ ment, 26% ultimately had surgery (mean 31 h after randomization) due most commonly to rebleeding or neurological deterioration. Principal findings: No significant benefit with surgery; trend toward a benefit with surgery for ICH ≤1 cm from cortical surface. 1. Mean ICH volume: 37–40 mL. 2. Favorable outcome: (a) Early surgery: 26%. (b) Initial conservative treatment: 24% (p = 0.4140). 3. Mortality: (a) Early surgery: 36%. (b) Initial conservative treatment: 37% (p = 0.707). 4. The only subgroup with a suggestion of ben­ efit with early surgery includes patients with a clot ≤1 cm of the cortical surface (odds ratio for a favorable outcome: 0.69; 95% CI 0.47–1.01). 17 Intracerebral Hemorrhage

passages.text: SECTION 8 Pediatrics 2044 multiple targets can be safely interrogated with this technique. Surgical resection of a single or multiple epileptogenic tubers is an established and effective treatment of children with TS and medically refractory seizures.71,72 Limited experience with LITT has been compiled for treatment of tubers with mixed results, and follow-up times are limited in those patients who have had beneficial results, who are still in the minority among the few dozen patients accumulated.57-59 We have also used neuromodulatory approaches to augment standard resective approaches in TS, using responsive neurostimulation (RNS) in the dominant motor area with good success in a TS case refractory to medical and ablative surgical treatments. Other structural etiologies amenable to resection include vascular lesions, with cavernous malformations, arteriovenous malformations, and gliotic injury caused by early childhood stroke, and all can lead to potential cures with surgical intervention through similar single or staged approaches. Although children are rarely affected by ischemic, embolic, or hemorrhagic cerebrovascular accidents, up to two-thirds may develop epilepsy after perinatal strokes and hypoxic or ischemic injury, and 25% of those will develop intractable epilepsy. In this population, treatment options range from focal to multilobar resections, disconnections, and even hemispherotomies, most effectively guided by intracranial electrode monitoring.73 Cavernous malformations are epileptogenic lesions commonly found in the pediatric population. Epilepsy is more common when the cavernoma is cortical as opposed to subcortical. Other risk factors for epilepsy include temporal localization, multiple cavernomas, volume of the cavernoma, volume of the hemosiderin ring, and male sex. Surgical decision making can be guided by correlating semiology with electrodiagnostic findings, and removal of the lesion and the hemosiderin ring and early intervention are advocated based on safety and expected efficacy.74 We feel that adjunctive invasive monitoring should be used in cases of longstanding intractability, because adjacent cortical and allocortical structures can often become epileptogenic independently,75 although extended resection compared with lesionectomy alone has been called into question in a meta- analysis76 and more recent series.77 Hemispherotomy and Disconnections Holohemispheric dysfunction and the plasticity of the develop- ing brain make functional hemispherotomy a safe, effective, and occasionally indicated technique in pediatric epilepsy surgery.78 Disorders such as the inflammatory Rasmussen encephalitis (RE), extensive structural injury due to early childhood stroke, the struc- tural migrational disorder hemimegalencephaly, and the genetic Sturge-Weber syndrome are curable through a hemispheric discon- nection achieved using intraventricular disruption of the descend- ing outputs of the temporal, parietal, and frontal lobes, a frontal disconnection, complete corpus callosotomy, and insular topec- tomy.79,80 Endoscopic-assisted approaches have been described,81 to overcome the invasiveness and potential side effects such as hydrocephalus and superficial hemosiderosis that can be seen after larger exposures and resections.79,80,82 RE is a rare chronic inflammatory epilepsy and progressive disorder. The neurological deficits are progressive and include hemiparesis, cognitive decline, and even aphasia (if the dominant hemisphere is affected). Children with RE have severe unilateral focal epilepsy, and often epilepsia partialis continua. Although MRI findings are often normal in the early stages of the disease, focal cortical swelling can be noted in some patients early in the disease, but the hallmark of the disease is progressive hemispheric atrophy. Surgical treatment is most commonly hemispherotomy. Although it is effective in arresting the potentially catastrophic hemispheric epilepsies of infancy and early childhood, a long- term study showed only a palliative effect from hemispherotomy/ hemispherectomy in RE, with only 22% of patients achieving seizure freedom at 10 years postoperatively, and considerable morbidity with speech and motor deficits83; a quest for less invasive immunomodulatory and neuromodulatory strategies is necessary. passages.text: 433 35 • Supraorbital Approach Variants for Intracranial Tumors prevent scarring in the eyebrow or a cosmetic compro- mise from weakness in the frontal branch of the facial nerve. After incision, the orbicularis muscle is dissected. Once the tissues are mobilized with elastic retraction, an en-bloc craniotomy is performed with the following bor- ders: median line–supraorbital foramen; external line–the zygomatic-orbital joint; and in the lower border–the orbital arch and 1-cm extension into the depth of the orbital roof until the intracranial portion is found. An en-bloc resec- tion is made at the superior margin of the frontal 1.5-cm craniotomy, after removing the periosteum and protect- ing the orbital fat. The orbital bone is drilled with different boundaries and choices according to the surgical target. This procedure allows us to work in the intraorbital space, the intracranial–extradural space, and the intracranial– subarachnoid space.25,26 Once the drilling of the inner edges of the craniotomy is completed, the dura mater is opened under the microscope and the appropriate microsurgical corridor is dissected. The trans-supraorbital approach provides access to the entire sellar region and its neurovascular structures, provid- ing enough space for surgical manipulation in cases of pitu- itary tumors with extrasellar extension to the optic chiasm. In selected cases, endoscopy has been used39-49 (Aesculap, Ventriculoscope, Tuttlingen, Germany, 0 and 30 degrees) to confirm patency of neighboring arteries, explore the intra- sellar space and cavernous extension of the tumor, and finally, evaluate the pituitary stalk and sellar diaphragm (Fig. 35-9).3,4,25,26,33,39,57 Benefits and Limitations One of the main advantages of the minimally invasive concept used in neurosurgery is that it involves a strategy that preserves the anatomic integrity of all structures, inso- far as is possible, using a supraselective tailored approach. The latter should correlate specifically with the surgical target.3,4,25-28,33,50 Therefore, the approach does not entail a reductionist or minimalist vision, but rather one based on experience to find the proper balance in the design and size of the craniotomy aimed at achieving the surgical goal without subjecting the patient to unnecessary risk from tis- sue manipulation. A practical example is shown by the historical progres- sion from initially using large craniotomies to clip elective aneurysms to current use of tailored approaches. This pro- gression is based on two basic principles. First, consider- ing that microsurgery involves a 20-mm work space, it was concluded that resorting to greater exposure with the cra- niotomy was not strictly necessary (the evolution of a wide frontotemporal craniotomy to the pterional craniotomy). Second is inclusion anatomic integrity in the surgical plan, not only of cerebral structures with less retraction and manipulation, but also to preserve the craniofacial struc- tures as well (avoiding atrophy of the temporal muscle, temporomandibular dysfunction, bone defects, fistula, flap necrosis, etc.).5,10,36,47,48,58 Thus, minimally invasive neurosurgery is not a discipline but a neurosurgical concept that may result in less morbidity due to the rational use and support of technology as part of the armamentarium and current multidisciplinary options used for the patient’s benefit.38 That is the reason why seri- ous complications can result with a limited understanding of the principles of minimally invasive approaches and an overuse of technological support. Critical analysis of surgi- cal experience accumulated thus far has demonstrated that major complications with minimally invasive approaches can occur if microsurgical principles are not properly fol- lowed. The key is to maintain a proper balance between overexposure and limited/reduced exposure. passages.text: CHAPTER 98 Laser Interstitial Thermal Therapy in Epilepsy 633 98 epilepsy versus open surgical resection suggests comparable rates of seizure freedom between the two interventions (43% for LITT vs. 50% for open resection), with similar complication rates in the two groups.77 The reason for the lower success rate with LITT may be related to extent of ablation achievable compared with open surgery. The proportion of ablated insula as well as the ablated volume appear to correlate with positive seizure outcome.78 Overall, LITT provides a new treatment option for insular epilepsy that, although probably less effective than open surgery, has an acceptable success rate and perhaps a more favorable adverse effects profile. Tuberous sclerosis complex is a multisystem disease that is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion. Epilepsy is one of the most common clinical presentations of tuberous sclerosis complex and affects up to 90% of individuals. Medical management leads to control in around one-third of cases. The remainder are considered for surgical intervention if the tubers are surgically accessible. If associated with multiple tubers, single or staged resections can be arranged.79 In a large series of 74 patients, surgical intervention yielded an Engel class I outcome in 48 (65%) and 37 (50%) patients at 1- and 2-year follow-up, respectively. However, the authors also noted complications rates ranging from 2% to 5%, including neurological deficits, wound complications, and CNS infection.80,81 Although the experience of LITT with tuberous sclerosis complex is still limited, preliminary data suggest a benefit of seizure control, but not as high as that of surgical resection (see Table 98.2). Reasons behind the apparent lower rate of success with LITT compared with open surgery is likely related to the extent of ablation as studies have shown that greater extent of resection (more than just the tuber) is associated with higher likelihood of seizure freedom.81 Hypothalamic Hamartomas Hypothalamic hamartomas (HHs) are rare developmental mal- formations of the ventral hypothalamus that are associated with precocious puberty and gelastic seizures and are typically associ- ated with refractory epilepsy, usually resulting in complex partial seizures or gelastic seizures.85–87 Given their deep-seated and criti- cal location, HHs are an ideal target for LITT. They represent the most frequent indication for LITT in pediatric epilepsy.88 Open surgery before LITT included transcallosal, subfrontal, skull base, and endoscopic. With advancement of surgical tech- niques, seizure-free rates range from around 30% to 50% when accounting for the majority of large reports.86,89,90 Complication rates from surgical resection are not infrequent (2% to 50%), and serious complications such as transient hemiparesis, hormonal dis- turbances, memory impairment, stroke, and optic tract injury have been reported.89,90 Although still limited in number, more recent series reported that open surgical resection combined with intra- operative MRI allowed a greater extent of safer resection and a higher seizure freedom in up to 80% in some series, with a signifi- cantly lower complication rate compared with prior reports.91,92 A number of reports about MRgLITT for HH suggest marked efficacy and low morbidity using this approach (Fig. 98.3). In one of the largest studies, 97% were seizure free at 1 year follow-up, a significantly higher number than that reported with open surgical resection.93 Furthermore, LITT has a significantly more favorable safety profile than open/endoscopic resection for HH. For instance, memory loss after LITT is around 1.5% compared with higher rates (up to 15%) after surgical resection. passages.loc: Youmans and Winn Neurological Surgery — 300 - Radiosurgery for Intracranial Vascular Malformations, p.5363
passages.text: CHAPTER 396 Blast-Induced Neurotrauma 3123 396 1. Immediate prehospital care aims to prevent secondary brain inju- ry: this includes maintenance of airway; adequate ventilation; and correction of hypoxia, hypercapnia, and hypotension. Also, it is important to look for damage in other organs (such as lungs or gastrointestinal tract) and exclude polytrauma. 2. Urgent resuscitation should focus on preventing increase of ICP and maintaining optimal brain oxygenation; the recommend- ed treatment approaches are addressed in other chapters. The rationale for using hyperosmotic agents is that the increased osmolarity of the blood allows the fluid from the extravascular space to enter the intravascular space; this in turn decreases brain edema, improves cerebral blood flow, and decreases CSF production.184-186 There is no clear consensus about the hyperosmotic agent most preferable in these patients: while some sources recommend administration of hypertonic saline, others recommend mannitol, especially if the patients are not adequately resuscitated and there is a possibility of hyperchlo- remic acidosis. Excessive crystalloid administration, permis- sive hypotension, hypoxia, and hypercapnia should be avoided owing to their potentially harmful effects.179 Early correction of traumatic coagulopathy is achieved with combinations of blood, blood products (fresh-frozen plasma, platelets), cryo- precipitate, and prothrombin complex concentrate. 3. Surgical interventions include craniotomy to evacuate compres- sive mass lesions and decompressive craniectomy to prevent hernia- tion and reduce ICP that results from severe brain edema.187-189 During Operation Iraqi Freedom and Operation Enduring Freedom, most injuries were inflicted by explosive weaponry, shifting the paradigm of combat injuries from gunshot wounds to blast injuries. The modus operandi of combat casualty care both in Iraq and in Afghanistan followed this paradigm shift by adopting an aggressive approach for medical evacuation.190 The injured soldiers often received initial treatment and resus- citation at a forward surgical station and were then transported quickly to a large base such as Balad in Iraq, where hospital, CT, and neurosurgical capabilities existed. Patients underwent initial neurosurgical treatment there including craniotomy, decompressive craniectomy, placement of ICP monitors, and stabilization. Often after 24 to 72 hours of initial treatment and stabilization in theater, military personnel were then air trans- ported out of theater on a specially equipped large air trans- porter aircraft (Critical Care Air Transport) to the US military Landstuhl Regional Medical Center in Germany where they received care in the Level I trauma center and intensive care unit. When further stabilized and ready for transport, patients were air transferred again by Critical Care Air Transport teams to US military hospitals stateside. The severely injured soldiers, after being resuscitated at the combat support hospital, were transported by air transport with critical care capabilities to the place of definitive care. As a necessity, the procedure of rapid, far-forward cranial decompression had been introduced to pre- vent malignant cerebral swelling and increased ICP during the flight. Indeed, it has been shown that early decompression facil- itated safe aeromedical evacuation of casualties. In high-energy trauma with contamination and soft tissue destruction, surgery was conducted with regard for future soft tissue reconstruction. In the military setting, bone flaps were discarded, and when cra- nioplasty was performed, patient-specific implants were used months later to correct the cranial defects. Two patients had Merland type II perimedullary fistulas and the remaining eight patients had Merland type III perimedullary fistulas. Sixteen embolization procedures were performed: 14 via transarterial routes, and 2 via transvenous routes. Complete occlusion was achieved in seven of the patients, incomplete occlusion was achieved in two patients, and one patient refused follow-up angiography. One patient suf- fered transient worsening of paraplegia after rupture of the anterior spinal artery by a detachable balloon. The mean follow-up period was 44.8 months (range, 3 to 112 months). Type II and type III spinal cord AVMs are characteristi- cally difficult to treat via conventional microsurgical and endovascular techniques. Hence, there has been significant interest in the use of stereotactic radiosurgery to treat these lesions. Stereotactic radiosurgery is thought to induce grad- ual endothelial hyperplasia, which eventually causes vessel narrowing and occlusion. Initial reluctance to use stereotac- tic radiosurgery to treat spinal cord vascular malformations stemmed from concern for spinal cord injury secondary to radiation toxicity. Sinclair and colleagues reported a series of 15 patients with intramedullary spinal cord AVMs treated with multisession cyberknife radiosurgery.35 Multisession radiosurgery was employed with the hope that allowing time between sessions would permit normal tissue repair, thus increasing the dose that could be administered to the AVM nidus because of increased radiation tolerance of the adjacent normal spinal cord. Patients were treated with an average marginal dose of 20.5 Gr over two to five sessions. Clinical and radiographic (MRI) follow-up were performed annually. Spinal angiography was repeated after 3 years. The mean follow-up period was 27.9 months (range, 3 to 59 months). Of the seven patients who were 3 years or more from treatment, six had significant reductions in the size of their lesions. Of the five patients who underwent post- treatment spinal angiograms, one patient showed complete obliteration of a conus medullaris AVM. No patient suffered a hemorrhage or neurologic deterioration after cyberknife treatment. Sinclair and colleagues were thus able to dem- onstrate that stereotactic radiosurgery for the treatment of intramedullary spinal cord AVMs is safe and feasible, but more studies are needed to further validate this approach. Conclusion Significant advances have been made in the treatment of spi- nal vascular malformations and fistulas during the last cen- tury. A more comprehensive understanding of the anatomy and pathophysiology of these lesions has led to a clearer classification system, which in turn has improved our abil- ity to appropriately categorize these lesions. Advances in diagnostic neuroradiology have facilitated lesion classifica- tion and has reduced the need in many cases for extensive spinal angiograms. The current treatment of spinal dural AVFs involves the integration of multiple techniques includ- ing microsurgery, endovascular surgery, and radiosurgery. Future directions depend on further refinements in treat- ment technologies. In open surgery, minimally invasive approaches might add benefit. For endovascular therapy, further refinements in biplane angiography and its integra- tion with other modalities such as MRI hold great promise. Better embolic agents that might play a biological role or sensitize a lesion to radiation may be the next revolution- ary advances. Radiosurgery is perhaps the least explored modality for spinal vascular malformations and will likely see a growing role in the next decade. Skull Base Reconstruction Meticulous attention to reconstruction of the skull base is focused on the separation of intradural and sinonasal cavities. The primary goals of skull base reconstruction are to repair dural defects, prevent CSF fistula, and segregate the intracranial and sinonasal cavities. Fail- ure to do so may result in life-threatening infectious complications. With transnasal procedures, watertight dural closure (if feasible) and multilayer “gasket-seal” closure with superimposed vascularized pedicled nasoseptal flap are important for closure of large skull base defects. Outcome, Durability, and Rate of Recurrence Despite the initial enthusiasm for aggressive surgical resection, particu- larly for lesions extending to the cavernous sinus, surgeons have encoun- tered significant procedure-related neurological morbidity. Therefore, various surgeons have more recently advocated subtotal resection fol- lowed by close surveillance; their studies demonstrated an acceptable risk/benefit ratio in long-term follow-up. They also observed similar recurrence rates in gross total and subtotal resection cases. The use of SRS as either primary or adjuvant modality has also revealed excellent tumor control rates with an acceptable side-effect profile for cavernous lesions in particular. In a recent meta-analysis of 2,000 patients with cavernous meningi- oma, Sughrue et al demonstrated the superiority of radiation treatment over surgical resection in preserving cranial nerve function. Typically, SRS or stereotactic radiation therapy offer tumor control rates in excess of 90% through 5-year follow-up for small- and medium-sized meningi- omas. For extracavernous meningiomas, surgical resection offers similar outcome results. A primary caveat to tumor response rates with SRS is the grade of meningioma. Stafford et al demonstrated 5-year tumor con- trol rates of 93, 68, and 0% for benign, atypical, and malignant subtypes, highlighting the paramount importance of histological diagnosis in sus- picious and rapidly growing lesions (supports algorithm steps 4 and 6). HPCs have an inevitable tendency for local recurrence (up to 91%) and distant metastasis (15-year risk of distant metastasis ~70%) after sur- gery alone. Therefore, radiological surveillance for systemic disease is recommended. The average time to local recurrence increased from 34 to 75 months and overall survival extended from 62 to 92 months with adjuvant RT compared with surgery alone. HPC has a dose-dependent response to fractionated RT, with dosage >45 Gy showing superior local control. Adjuvant SRS (~15 Gy, single dose) also has good local tumor control (80%), without any protection for distant metastasis. Other important prognostic factors for recurrence are tumor grade and extent of resection. In a series of 15 patients with CSH (median tumor volume 29.3 mL) who were treated using 13.4 Gy median marginal dose of SRS, Anqi et al observed a reduction in tumor size in 90% of patients and an improve- ment in cranial neuropathies in 87% at an average follow-up of 13 months (supports algorithm steps 4, 5, and 7). For extradural transcav- ernous resection of large tumors, gross total resection is feasible in ~92% of patients, ensuring complete remission in long run (supports algorithm steps 4 and 6). Recurrence rates for JNA are ~20 to 30% for primary RT alone. There- fore, surgical resection is beneficial for most lesions for optimal out- come. Multimodality management offers 91.7, 70.7, and 70.7% progres- sion-free survival for patients with Radkowski’s stage III JNA at 3, 5, and 10 years, respectively (supports algorithm steps 5 and 6). Complete excision of paraganglioma can be achieved in approxi- mately 78% of patients, ensuring high long-term tumor control rates of approximately 96%. passages.text: SECTION 8 Pediatrics 1732 manifest as secondary compression from a tumor or cyst or as an isolated cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle.20 Success following an ETV is defined by amelioration of the signs and symptoms of elevated intracranial pressure (ICP), with reported rates ranging from 42% to 90%.21–28 Factors that influence the outcome include etiology of hydrocephalus, age, prior treatment history, and anatomic factors. Specifically, age younger than 6 months and prior history of communicating hydrocephalus (particularly postinfectious hydrocephalus in North American series) or shunt insertion are associated with poor outcomes,27,28 while preoperative thinning and inferior bowing of the third ventricle floor are associated with ETV success.17 Scarring in the prepontine cistern can be seen on MRI dedicated to sagittal images of this region such as T2-weighted sequences, particularly fast imaging employing steady-state acquisition (FIESTA) or constructive interference in steady-state (CISS) sequences. When seen, this scarring portends poor outcome after ETV. In general, insufficient expansion of the subarachnoid spaces contributes to a high rate of ETV failure in young infants. If the third ventricular floor anatomy appears less favorable for safe ventriculostomy, fenestration of the lamina terminalis occasionally provides an alternative channel. There are several factors that predict success of a repeat ETV after initial failed ETV. Repeat ETV is favored in the setting of a closed aqueduct and unscarred basal cisterns (particularly the prepontine cistern) on thin-cut T2-weighted FIESTA or CISS MRI, or on intraoperative inspection.29 In that analysis, factors that are associated with increased rate of successful revision ETV include durability of the initial ETV for more than 3 months (particularly if more than 6 months), previous attempt at choroid plexus cauterization (CPC), and postinfectious hydrocephalus etiology. Revision ETV may be successful in one-half of patients, with the greatest rates in patients with the longest time to failure of the primary ETV. The limited success rate of ETV in young patients for noncommunicating hydrocephalus prompted the development of combined ETV+CPC, with promising results in some series examining aqueductal stenosis, intraventricular hemorrhage of prematurity, Dandy-Walker malformation, myelomeningocele, and infection.5,16,30–35 The basic concept of CPC is premised on the attempt to control CSF production by ablating the choroid plexus where CSF is produced. Variations in success rate across different series of ETV combined with CPC speak to the critical importance of patient selection, technical nuances, and recognition of factors associated with failure.36 Although there are many techniques, the largest and most successful contemporary studies have used a flexible endoscope system and meticulous cauterization of the entirety of the bilateral choroid plexii.30,37 Some observational studies suggest that maximal CPC is a key part of the procedure’s learning curve and is associated with improved outcomes in the literature,38,39 although this has not been replicated in all studies.37,40 In a 2019 multicenter study conducted by the Hydrocephalus Clinical Research Network, of 191 patients who underwent primary ETV+CPC, young age (<1 month) and an etiology of post–intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH) secondary to prematurity were independent predictors of ETV+CPC failure.37 Even when patients are shunt independent after successful ETV+CPC, they may have a more modest reduction in CSF volumes. The neurocognitive outcomes of this phenomenon were compared during a randomized trial in Uganda that randomized infants with postinfectious hydrocephalus to ETV+CPC versus shunting.41 Although shunt placement reduced CSF volume compared with ETV+CPC, cognitive outcomes and rate of brain growth did not differ between groups. Cognitive outcome correlated with brain volume, but not ventricular volume. At this time, it is believed that the greater CSF volume after ETV+CPC does not affect cognitive outcome. Further supporting the conclusions of ARUBA, the Scottish Intracranial Vascular Malformation Study (SIVMS), using data from the Scottish Audit of Intracranial Vascular Malformations, performed a population-based observational cohort study of 204 patients in whom an AVM was diagnosed.9 Of those patients, 103 underwent intervention (any endovascular embolization, neurosurgical excision, or stereotactic radiosurgery alone or in combination). Outcomes were compared between the two groups at 4 years and after 4 years. The investigators found that the rate of the primary outcome (death or sustained morbidity) was worse among the intervention recipients at 4 years but equal in the two groups after 4 years. They also found that the rate of the secondary outcome (nonfatal symptomatic stroke or death from the AVM, associated aneurysm, or intervention) was worse among the intervention recipients at all time points. This study is weakened predominantly by its design as a nonrandomized, observational cohort study. Considerable time has passed since the interventions conducted as part of ARUBA and SIVMS. Both endovascular techniques to completely embolize AVMs and the devices used in these procedures have improved considerably in the intervening years. The results of these studies may be outdated, and further investigation taking into account recent technological and procedural advances detailed later in this chapter is warranted. Wu and colleagues published a systematic-review of 15 studies written before 2018 detailing the treatment of 597 patients and 598 AVMs treated with endovascular therapy for cure. Complete obliteration was seen in 58.3% of all cases; the procedural complication rate was 13.6% and the overall clinical complication rate was 24.1%.10 When primary embolization was used to treat highly selected patients, complete obliteration was obtained at a rate of higher than 90%; however, the authors commented that the high rate of complications associated with embolization with intent to cure might shift the risk-benefit equation in favor of conservative management. They do conclude that carefully selected patients with lesions favorable to treatment are valid candidates for curative endovascular therapy. Despite the ARUBA trial, the conclusions of the SIVMS, and this recent systematic analysis, the debate over the best manage- ment for AVM patients continues. Most neurologists and neuro- surgeons agree that because of the heterogeneity of brain AVMs, the data must be applied generally and judgment should be used in each particular case to determine which treatment strategy would be optimal for each patient. A treatment plan should be deter- mined by a multidisciplinary team that includes a neurologist, a neurointerventionalist, a radiation oncologist, and a cerebrovascu- lar neurosurgeon. In the majority of cases, endovascular emboliza- tion is the first-line treatment with the goal of decreasing the size of the nidus or the number of arterial feeders, or both, in anticipa- tion of completing treatment with microsurgical resection or, if the lesion is small enough, stereotactic radiosurgery. The role of endovascular embolization, however, has been expanding because of increasing evidence that embolization as monotherapy—facili- tated by the development of better embolic agents, catheters, and techniques—may be curative in patients with certain lesion char- acteristics. The goal of this chapter is to outline the evidence to date that endovascular embolization can be curative of a subset of AVMs and to define the patient characteristics, outcomes, and complications associated with this strategy.
